name: Halcyon
intro: A calm-tech identity for a sound-hardware company — a system built to breathe.
overview: Halcyon makes high-fidelity listening hardware. The brief was to make a technology brand feel like an instrument: precise, warm, and unhurried.
We built the identity around a single modulating mark and a motion grammar tied to waveform — every transition eases like sound settling into a room.

name: Pavilion
intro: Art direction for a temporary architecture biennale staged across a harbour.
overview: Pavilion invited twelve studios to build temporary structures along Copenhagen's waterfront. Our role was the visual world that tied them together.
A wayfinding-led system used scale and shadow as the primary devices — photography shot at dawn to catch the long light the structures were designed for.

name: Nocturne
intro: Main titles and identity for a nocturnal psychological thriller.
overview: Nocturne needed a title sequence that felt like memory under pressure — legible, then not.
Type dissolves into grain and reforms; the mark is a single serif N that behaves like a light source moving behind fog.

name: Meridian
intro: A 320-page monograph on contemporary Nordic landscape photography.
overview: Meridian collects two decades of landscape work into a single volume. The design had to disappear behind the images while holding a strong spine of typography.
A modular grid lets full-bleed plates breathe against quiet captions; three paper stocks separate the book's movements.

name: Verso
intro: Identity for an independent publisher of translated fiction.
overview: Verso publishes fiction in translation. The mark plays on the recto/verso of a page — a flippable monogram that reads both ways.
A flexible spine system lets every title share a shelf while keeping its own voice.

name: Saltfield
intro: A launch campaign for a coastal outerwear label, built in CG and grain.
overview: Saltfield's first season needed weather. We built CG environments — fog, salt, low sun — and dropped the product into them.
The result sits between product film and landscape painting; stills were pulled directly from the render for print.

name: Atlas Index
intro: A living index of a research institute's archive, rendered in WebGL.
overview: Atlas needed its public archive to feel alive rather than filed. We built a navigable index where entries drift and cluster by relation.
A custom WebGL layer handles the field of entries at 60fps; everything degrades gracefully to a static index without JS.

name: Low Tide
intro: A self-initiated photographic study of the North Sea at its lowest tide.
overview: Low Tide is a year of returning to the same stretch of coast at the lowest water of each month.
The edit became a small book and an exhibition — a study in how little needs to change for everything to.

name: Aperture
intro: Identity for a precision cinema-lens manufacturer.
overview: Aperture grinds lenses for cinema. The mark is an iris that opens across applications.
A monochrome system keeps focus on the optics themselves.

name: Field Notes
intro: A quarterly journal of fieldwork and design research.
overview: Field Notes gathers essays from designers working outside the studio.
A utilitarian grid and a single accent keep the writing foremost.

name: Cinder
intro: Packaging for a small-batch coffee roaster.
overview: Cinder roasts dark. The packaging leans into char — matte black with a single burned mark.
A tactile, unbleached stock carries the range across origins.

name: Murmur
intro: A motion identity for a conversation-led podcast network.
overview: Murmur needed an identity you could hear. The mark animates to voice — a waveform that gathers and disperses.
A toolkit of loops lets each show brand itself within the system.

name: Halo
intro: Identity for an architectural lighting studio.
overview: Halo designs light for buildings. The mark is a soft ring that behaves like a fixture across surfaces.
Warm neutrals and generous space let the work glow.

name: Quartz
intro: Spatial and graphic design for a mineralogy exhibition.
overview: Quartz brought a museum's mineral collection into the light for the first time in decades.
Vitrine typography and a crystalline wayfinding system guided visitors through formation, structure and use.
